mnemonics.title: SPUD - The Big Four
mnemonics.content: SPUD = Selection, Projection, Union, Difference - These are the 4 basic operations. Remember: 'Some Pies Under Different' - S(election), P(rojection), U(nion), D(ifference)

mnemonics.title: The Six Pack
mnemonics.content: Basic operations: σ Selection, π Projection, ∪ Union, − Difference, × Cartesian Product, ρ Rename. Mnemonic: 'Silly Professors Under Delightful Rainy Xmas' - Each first letter matches an operation

mnemonics.title: Join Family
mnemonics.content: Natural Join (⋈) + Theta Join (⋈θ) + Division (÷). Remember: 'Join Division Ceremony' - The family grows with each join type!

mnemonics.title: Operator Precedence
mnemonics.content: Precedence order: σ, π, ×, ∩, ∪, − (bottom to top). Mnemonic: 'Selecting Pies Xray In Unique Design' - operators ranked from lowest to highest precedence

mnemonics.title: Selection vs Projection
mnemonics.content: Selection (σ) = Horizontal slicing (rows), like choosing which rows to keep. Projection (π) = Vertical slicing (columns), like choosing which columns to display. Remember: 'Sigma Slices Rows, Pi Picks Columns'

acronyms.usage: The formal query language for relational databases - use when discussing the theoretical foundation of SQL
Filters rows based on condition - σcondition(Relation). Use when you need horizontal subset of tuples
Selects specific columns - πcolumn1,column2(Relation). Use when you need vertical subset of attributes
Renames relations or attributes - ρnewName(Relation). Use to avoid naming conflicts in complex queries
Joins relations on common attributes - R ⋈ S. Use when joining tables with matching attribute names
Join with condition - R ⋈θ S. Use when you need specific join conditions beyond equality
Inverse of Cartesian Product - R ÷ S. Use when finding tuples in R that relate to ALL tuples in S

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spreadsheet with a filter icon - you're physically filtering out rows you don't want, keeping only rows that satisfy the condition. Picture a funnel with data going in and filtered results coming out.
Think of a movie projector (π looks like projector) casting only certain columns onto the screen. You're projecting a spotlight on specific columns while others remain in darkness.
Picture a multiplication grid or times table - every row from table A combines with every row from table B, creating all possible combinations like a grid of intersections.
Imagine two venn diagrams overlapping - the common area represents joined tuples. Picture two puzzle pieces clicking together where they share matching attributes (the 'key' pieces).
Think of a set collection bag - you pour in all items from both sets, but only keep unique items (no duplicates). Visualize two circles combining into one larger circle.
Picture a subtraction mat - you have set A on one side, set B on the other. Subtract B from A means removing all elements of B from A. Imagine physically erasing overlapping elements.

analogies.analogy: Like using a filter in a coffee maker - coffee passes through, grounds stay behind. Or like a security gate checking IDs - only those with valid credentials (condition) pass through.
Like a photo projector showing only certain slides from a larger presentation on the wall. Or like reading only specific columns in an Excel sheet while hiding others.
Like generating all possible combinations for a sandwich order - every bread type combined with every filling type. Or like a deck of cards - 13 ranks × 4 suits = 52 combinations.
Like matching students to their ID cards - you find pairs where the student ID number matches the ID card number. Two separate lists become one matched list.
Like combining two lists of phone contacts from different phones and removing duplicates - you get one master list with no repeated entries.
Like removing all vegetarian items from a restaurant menu - you're left with only the non-vegetarian dishes. Or like subtracting your expenses from your income to find savings.
Like finding which teachers teach ALL subjects - if you have teacher-subject pairs and subject list, division finds teachers related to every subject. It's the inverse of 'all combinations'.
Like giving a nickname to someone in a group project to avoid confusion - you're not changing the person, just how they're referred to in the current context.
